full_response:
Let the two tunnels be T1 and T2 and assume (w.l.o.g.) that T1 is in fact the longer one.  
Each time we ask the device “Which tunnel is longer?”, it

 • answers “T1’’ (the correct answer) with probability p = 3/4, and  
 • answers “T2’’ (the wrong answer) with probability q = 1/4,

independently of all previous calls.

Algorithm (majority vote, constant number of calls)  
1. Call the device k times (k is an odd constant to be fixed).  
2. Let X be the number of times the device says “T1 is longer”.  
3. Output  
  T1 if X > k/2; otherwise output T2.

Because k is odd there is always a unique majority.

Analysis  
X ∼ Binomial(k , p).  
The algorithm is wrong exactly when X ≤ ⌊k/2⌋.  For an odd k this is “at most (k−1)/2 correct answers”, i.e. “at least (k+1)/2 wrong answers”.

Choose k = 7.

Probability of error

 P[ error ] = Σ_{r=4}^7 C(7 , r) q^{r} p^{7−r}

  = C(7,4) q⁴p³ + C(7,5) q⁵p² + C(7,6) q⁶p¹ + C(7,7) q⁷

  = 35·(1/4)⁴·(3/4)³ + 21·(1/4)⁵·(3/4)²  
    + 7·(1/4)⁶·(3/4)¹ + 1·(1/4)⁷

  = 35·(1/256)·(27/64)  + 21·(1/1024)·(9/16)  
    + 7·(1/4096)·(3/4) + 1·(1/16384)

  = 945/16384 + 189/16384 + 21/16384 + 1/16384  
  = 1156/16384 ≈ 0.0706.

Hence

 P[ algorithm correct ] = 1 − 0.0706 ≈ 0.9294 ≥ 0.9.

Therefore 7 independent uses of the device guarantee at least 90 % accuracy.

Why a constant always suffices  
For any odd k,

 P[ error ] = P[X ≤ k/2]  
            ≤ exp(−k/24)                (by a Chernoff bound)

which decreases exponentially in k.  Thus for every fixed target accuracy 1−ε we can achieve it with k = O(log(1/ε)) independent calls; in particular, ε = 0.1 needs only the constant k = 7 found above.

Cost  
Time: exactly 7 device calls = Θ(1).  
Space: Θ(1).

\boxed{\text{Call the device 7 times and take the majority; this yields correctness at least }0.9.}  

Confidence: 0.29
